text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
REASON FOR VISIT: Disease restaging review and treatment planning -- metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old retired industrial engineer presenting today for review of his most recent restaging CT scan obtained approximately 9 months after initiation of systemic immunotherapy for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation. Today's visit focuses on the interpretation of his imaging findings and discussion of next steps in management in the setting of confirmed nodal disease progression.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed with stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney approximately 9 months ago. At presentation, he had an 11.4 cm primary right renal mass with renal vein thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Pathology of the surgical specimen showed WHO/ISUP grade 4 ccRCC with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 22% of viable tumor volume and extensive tumor necrosis. NGS (Foundation One CDx) identified VHL frameshift, PBRM1 and BAP1 loss-of-function mutations, SETD2 loss, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (copy number 6.8), and TP53 p.R248W. TMB was low (6.2 mut/Mb), MSS. PD-L1 CPS was 18 by 22C3.

He underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately one month after diagnosis, with R0 resection of the primary and 3/8 positive retroperitoneal nodes (ypT3bN1). Four weeks post-operatively, he initiated first-line systemic immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles (per CheckMate 214 dosing schema), followed by transition to nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance therapy.

His disease course on immunotherapy has been characterized by a discordant/mixed response pattern: pulmonary target lesions responded meaningfully (right lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.7-0.8 cm), while retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease showed progressive growth. This divergent behavior was discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board at the 6-month mark, with concern that the sarcomatoid component of the tumor -- known to harbor distinct immunobiological behavior -- may be driving progression at nodal sites while the conventional clear cell component responds at pulmonary sites.

His most recent restaging CT (obtained approximately 9 months after immunotherapy initiation) now clearly demonstrates progressive nodal disease meeting radiographic criteria for progression: the right common iliac nodal mass has grown from 2.0 cm to 3.1 cm; the aortocaval conglomerate from 2.4 cm to 3.4 cm; the right obturator node from 1.1 cm to 1.8 cm; and there is a new right external iliac node measuring 1.2 cm. The pulmonary nodules appear stable (no further shrinkage, but no new lesions). There are no new hepatic, osseous, or CNS metastases on this scan.

Symptomatically, the patient reports new onset bilateral lower extremity swelling, particularly in the lower legs and ankles, which has developed over the past several weeks. He attributes this to the pelvic nodal burden obstructing lymphatic return. He also describes new, dull pelvic discomfort/pressure, most pronounced on the right side, and worsening fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks. He denies fever, chills, frank hematuria (the nephrectomy resolved this), dysuria, bowel changes, or neurological symptoms. He has not noted any new bone pain.

He remains on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily for immune-related hypothyroidism that developed after cycle 2 of ipi/nivo. His thyroid function has been well-controlled and he denies symptoms of hypothyroidism.

He is accompanied today by his wife, who confirms his symptom history and notes that his fatigue has significantly limited his ability to participate in usual activities over the past month.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

- Constitutional: Fatigue, grade 2, worsening over 4-6 weeks. Approximately 5 lb weight loss since prior visit (now 188 lbs, down from 198 lbs at diagnosis). Denies fever or night sweats.
- HEENT: No headaches. No vision changes. No oral ulcers or mucositis.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with climbing stairs (unchanged). No cough, hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: New bilateral lower extremity edema, grade 1, pitting, to mid-calf. No chest pain, palpitations.
- Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting. Normal bowel habits. No abdominal pain. No jaundice.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria. Mild urinary frequency, no dysuria. No new gross abnormalities.
- Musculoskeletal: Right-sided pelvic pressure/discomfort. No focal bone pain. No arthralgias.
- Neurological: No focal deficits. No headaches. No confusion.
- Skin: No rash. No immune-related skin changes currently.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. No adrenal insufficiency symptoms.
- Lymphatic: Patient aware of pelvic lymphadenopathy; reports sensation of heaviness in the right groin area.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/84 mmHg (controlled on current antihypertensives; baseline lisinopril 10 mg daily). Heart rate 78 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. Temperature 37.1°C. Weight 188 lbs. SpO2 97% on room air.

General: Alert and oriented, appears fatigued but in no acute distress. Well-groomed, cooperative.

HEENT: No scleral icterus. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Oral mucosa moist and intact without ulceration.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No JVD.

Abdomen: Soft. Mild right lower quadrant fullness without distinct palpable mass. Nephrectomy surgical site is well-healed. No hepatosplenomegaly. No peritoneal signs. Mild diffuse lower abdominal tenderness to deep palpation.

Lower Extremities: Bilateral 1+ pitting edema to mid-calf, slightly more pronounced on the right. No erythema, warmth, or tenderness to suggest DVT, though this would warrant further evaluation if clinically indicated.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy on exam (deep pelvic nodes not accessible on external examination).

Neurologic: Grossly intact. No focal deficits.

Skin: No rash. No immune-related dermatitis.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7.4 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable trend)
- Platelets: 312 K/uL (within normal limits; thrombocytosis has resolved post-nephrectomy)
- ANC: 4.8 K/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (stable; mildly elevated from solitary kidney; baseline post-nephrectomy ~1.6-1.8)
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Glucose: 118 mg/dL (mild hyperglycemia; patient on metformin for T2DM)
- Calcium: 9.8 mg/dL (normal; hypercalcemia has resolved since nephrectomy)
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- ALT: 32 U/L (normal)
- AST: 28 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 88 U/L (normal)
- LDH: 319 U/L (mildly elevated; previously 287 U/L at diagnosis -- modest rise consistent with disease progression)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (therapeutic, within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Coagulation:
- INR: 1.0, PT: 12.1 sec (normal)

Urinalysis: No gross hematuria. Trace protein, consistent with solitary kidney and chronic kidney disease stage 2.

---

IMAGING REVIEW:

Today's visit includes formal review of the CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained at the 9-month mark, which I personally reviewed alongside the radiology report.

Pulmonary: The bilateral pulmonary nodules appear largely stable compared to the prior scan. The right lower lobe nodule measures approximately 0.8 cm (unchanged from 0.7 cm at 6-month scan), the left lower lobe nodule is 0.8 cm, and the right upper lobe nodule is 0.3 cm. These have clearly responded from their baseline measurements (right lower lobe was 1.4 cm at diagnosis). The lungs look favorable -- the pulmonary disease, which was the main target lesion burden, has responded. No new pulmonary nodules.

Retroperitoneal/Pelvic Nodal Disease: This is where we are seeing the problem. The right common iliac nodal mass has grown substantially -- now 3.1 cm, up from 2.0 cm at the 6-month scan. The aortocaval nodal conglomerate is 3.4 cm (was 2.4 cm). The right obturator node is now 1.8 cm, up from 1.1 cm. There is also a new right external iliac node at 1.2 cm that was not present previously. This pattern of nodal progression in the pelvis and para-aortic region, with growth at multiple sites and new nodal involvement, is not something we can attribute to pseudoprogression at this point -- we gave it extra time at the 6-month review and the disease has continued to move in the wrong direction at nodal sites.

Bone/Other: No hepatic lesions, no osseous lesions, no CNS disease on this scan. The adrenal bed (right adrenalectomy site) is clear.

Overall impression: Progressive nodal disease on nivolumab maintenance. The pulmonary component is stable/responding, but the nodal disease has outpaced this response. We are dealing with a discordant pattern that has now crossed into unambiguous overall progression. The pelvic nodal burden is also now causing symptomatic lymphedema, which is directly affecting his quality of life.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with stage IV clear cell RCC, WHO/ISUP grade 4 with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), who has experienced a discordant response to first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab induction followed by nivolumab maintenance. His pulmonary disease responded well (and remains controlled), but he now has unambiguous progressive pelvic and retroperitoneal nodal disease with associated symptomatic lymphedema. His LDH has modestly risen, and his performance status has declined from ECOG 1 to ECOG 2, driven largely by fatigue and lower extremity edema.

This represents radiographic and clinical progression on PD-1 checkpoint blockade therapy. He has received a total of approximately 9 months of immunotherapy.

1. DISEASE STATUS -- PROGRESSIVE DISEASE ON FIRST-LINE IMMUNOTHERAPY:
We have confirmed progression on nivolumab maintenance. Given the consistent pattern of nodal growth over multiple scans (3 imaging time points showing progressive nodal enlargement, new nodal sites), this is definitively true progression, not pseudoprogression. Notably, his sarcomatoid dedifferentiation (22% at surgical specimen, CPS 22 by 22C3 in sarcomatoid areas) is a double-edged feature: it can initially respond to immunotherapy but can also be a source of acquired resistance, particularly when driven by BAP1 loss (which this patient has) and CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, both of which are associated with aggressive tumor biology.

2. TRANSITION TO SECOND-LINE THERAPY -- CABOZANTINIB:
After review and discussion with the patient and his wife, we will discontinue nivolumab and initiate second-line therapy with cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg orally once daily as standard starting dose.

Rationale for cabozantinib as second-line choice:
- Cabozantinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR1/2/3, MET, AXL, RET, and other targets. The METEOR trial established its superiority over everolimus in previously treated ccRCC with significant improvement in OS, PFS, and ORR.
- Critically for this patient, his NGS profile showed MET amplification (copy number 6.8). Cabozantinib directly targets MET kinase, and preclinical and clinical data suggest that MET-amplified tumors may be particularly sensitive to MET inhibition. This patient's nodal progression may be partly MET-driven, and cabozantinib is well-positioned to address this.
- AXL overexpression has been associated with sarcomatoid differentiation in RCC, and cabozantinib's AXL inhibitory activity makes it particularly attractive in this context.
- Given that the pulmonary disease responded to immunotherapy and there is residual immune benefit at pulmonary sites, we are choosing a VEGFR TKI rather than a combination approach at this time. Cabozantinib monotherapy is the appropriate standard second-line option.

Starting dose: 60 mg/day. Dose reductions to 40 mg/day and then 20 mg/day available for toxicity management per label.

Key side effects reviewed extensively with patient and his wife:
- Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Recommend starting urea 20% or lactic acid cream prophylactically; avoid tight footwear; inspect hands and feet daily; return immediately if blistering or inability to walk.
- Diarrhea: Begin loperamide at first sign of loose stools (4 mg initial dose, 2 mg after each loose stool); low-residue/BRAT diet guidance provided.
- Hypertension: He is already on lisinopril for baseline hypertension and his BP has been reasonably controlled. TKIs frequently cause or worsen hypertension through VEGF inhibition. We will increase lisinopril to 20 mg daily preemptively and monitor BPs closely (home BP log requested). He should call if BP consistently above 150/90.
- Fatigue and nausea: Common. Fatigue may overlap with his disease-related fatigue. Nausea usually mild; PRN antiemetics prescribed (ondansetron 4 mg as needed).
- Hypothyroidism: He already has immune-related hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. TKIs can also cause hypothyroidism (separate mechanism via thyroid inflammation/ischemia). Will monitor TSH monthly initially.
- Hepatotoxicity: LFTs will be monitored at each visit.
- Wound healing: No active surgical wounds. Patient has healed nephrectomy site, so not an immediate concern.
- QTc prolongation: Baseline EKG to be obtained today before first dose.
- Proteinuria: Urine protein/creatinine ratio to be checked at next visit.

Prescription provided for cabozantinib 60 mg (Cabometyx). Patient and family given written educational materials. Pharmacy contact information provided for questions.

3. ANTIHYPERTENSIVE ADJUSTMENT:
Lisinopril dose increased from 10 mg daily to 20 mg daily in anticipation of TKI-related hypertension. Patient to monitor BP at home and log readings. Return sooner if BP persistently elevated above 150/90 mmHg or if symptoms develop.

4. LOWER EXTREMITY EDEMA:
Likely lymphedema secondary to pelvic nodal disease. As the nodal disease responds to cabozantinib (anticipated), we expect this to improve. In the meantime, compression stockings (20-30 mmHg) recommended for bilateral lower extremities; referral to lymphedema therapy placed. We also need to rule out DVT given his cancer history and new edema -- lower extremity duplex ultrasound ordered today. If DVT confirmed, anticoagulation with LMWH or DOAC will be initiated.

5. ANEMIA:
Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, mildly low but stable trend. No active bleeding. Likely related to chronic disease and solitary kidney. Continue to monitor; no transfusion needed at this time. Iron studies to be checked at next visit.

6. GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT:
Fasting glucose 118 mg/dL; patient's T2DM is managed with metformin. Given his creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR estimated ~42 mL/min/1.73m2), creatinine needs monitoring -- metformin dose may need adjustment if renal function worsens. He was counseled to hold metformin if undergoing contrast-enhanced imaging and to stay well-hydrated.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
TSH 3.1 mIU/L -- therapeutic on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Continue current dose. Recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks given new TKI initiation which may further impact thyroid function.

8. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND QUALITY OF LIFE:
ECOG 2 today, driven by fatigue and lower extremity edema. I anticipate that if cabozantinib produces a response in the nodal disease, his symptoms should improve meaningfully. We will reassess performance status at next visit. Referral to palliative care placed for symptom management support and quality-of-life optimization -- this is not end-of-life care but supportive care to help him manage the cumulative burden of treatment side effects and disease symptoms.

9. FOLLOW-UP PLAN:
- Telephone follow-up or office visit in 2-3 weeks to assess tolerance of cabozantinib and review lower extremity ultrasound results.
- Lab work in 4 weeks: CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, urine protein/creatinine ratio.
- BP check: Patient to present to lab or call nurse line with home BP log in 2 weeks.
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months (at 3 months of cabozantinib therapy).
- Continue levothyroxine, metformin, and lisinopril (now 20 mg daily).
- EKG ordered today prior to first cabozantinib dose.

Time spent: 55 minutes total, including extensive counseling with patient and spouse. Greater than half the time was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Medical Oncologist]
Department of Medical Oncology
